content.text: is a unit of agent work that executes automatically at a fixed time, on a repeating interval, or according to a calendar expression — without a human sending a request. Instead of waiting for an inbound API call or a queue message, the scheduler itself acts as the trigger, waking the agent at the right moment.
In the agentic AI context, scheduled jobs bridge the gap between

. A scheduled job might run a research agent every morning to summarise overnight news, ingest a new batch of customer conversations into a vector store each hour, or sweep for anomalies in production metrics every five minutes. The agent runs the same logical workflow each time; the schedule is simply the trigger that initiates it.

Scheduling is distinct from event-driven triggers (where a message arrival starts the job) and on-demand calls (where a user or service starts the job synchronously). All three patterns can coexist in a single agent system.

Most production agent deployments need to do work that is not user-initiated. Business rhythms — end-of-day reports, hourly data ingestion, weekly digest emails — align to the clock, not to user clicks. Without a reliable scheduler, you must either leave these tasks unautomated or build ad-hoc trigger mechanisms (e.g., a human running a script each morning).

— agents that surface insights before users ask for them. A nightly churn-risk classifier, a weekly code-quality reviewer, or a daily competitive-intelligence gatherer all deliver value that a purely reactive agent cannot. The scheduler is the component that makes agents truly autonomous rather than just fast responders.
Jobs fire whether or not a user is present. Night-time batch processing, weekend data pipelines, and holiday-period monitoring continue without human supervision.
Knowing when jobs run lets you pre-allocate compute, batch LLM calls at off-peak hours to reduce cost, and stay within rate limits by spreading load.
A daily report that runs at 06:00 every morning builds user trust. Stakeholders know when to check for new outputs rather than polling an API or waiting for an ad-hoc run.
Long-running LLM workflows (5–20 minutes) are unsuitable for synchronous HTTP responses. Scheduling them as background jobs removes that constraint entirely.
A scheduled job can enqueue messages, start child workflows, or call APIs. It acts as the entry point into a broader multi-step agent pipeline.
If the scheduler process restarts during a scheduled window, the run may be skipped silently. You must decide whether missed runs should be backfilled or discarded.

A scheduled job fetches data at fire time. If the underlying data changes between the scheduled run and when a user reads the output, the report may already be stale. This is unavoidable in polling-based designs — schedule tighter intervals or switch to event-driven triggers where freshness is critical.

If a job takes longer than its interval (e.g., an hourly job that runs for 90 minutes), the next run starts while the previous one is still in progress. Without an explicit overlap policy, you get concurrent mutations, doubled LLM costs, and corrupted outputs.

Scheduled jobs run in the background with no user watching. An exception that would surface immediately in a synchronous call goes unnoticed for hours unless you instrument alerts and dead-letter queues.

A production scheduler requires persistent storage (to survive restarts), distributed locking (to prevent duplicate runs across replicas), and monitoring. This is non-trivial to build correctly from scratch.

If a job sends an email, inserts a row, or calls an external API and then crashes mid-run before recording success, a retry will duplicate those effects. Every scheduled job must be designed to be idempotent.
The execution flow of a scheduled agent job follows four stages:
The scheduler evaluates the clock against registered job schedules. When a job's cron expression or interval fires, the scheduler creates a
containing the job name, run ID, scheduled timestamp, and any payload parameters, then pushes it to a task queue.
A worker process polls the task queue, claims the task record (with a distributed lock or visibility timeout to prevent double-execution), and begins execution.

The worker runs the agent logic — loading context, calling the LLM, using tools, and persisting intermediate state. Long-running jobs send periodic heartbeats so the scheduler knows the job is alive and can timeout and retry dead workers.

On success, the worker records the run result (output artifact, status, duration) in a job history table or object store. On failure, it records the error and either retries with exponential back-off or sends the task to a dead-letter queue after exhausting retries.

attrs.alt: Scheduled Agent Job runtime architecture: Scheduler fires trigger into Task Queue, dispatched to Agent Worker (Load Context, LLM Inference, Actions, Persist), which writes to Outputs (DB, Email, Dashboard, Webhook, Audit log, Dead-letter queue)

content.text: Use scheduled jobs when the work has a natural time cadence that is independent of user requests:
Daily sales summaries, weekly KPI digests, or monthly compliance reports that stakeholders expect at a fixed time.
Hourly ingestion of new documents into a vector store, nightly ETL jobs that pull from external APIs, or incremental re-indexing of changed content.
An agent that checks system metrics or customer sentiment every 5–15 minutes and sends an alert only when something is out of bounds.
A weekly agent that reviews a user's recent activity and drafts personalised suggestions — e.g., code improvements, content ideas, or churn-risk interventions.
Cache warming, stale-record cleanup, embedding re-computation after a model upgrade, or quota reset logic.
Avoid scheduled jobs when freshness is the primary concern (prefer event-driven triggers) or when the work must finish within a user's request timeout (prefer synchronous serving).
— a pure-Python in-process scheduler — as the baseline. Later examples show Celery Beat for multi-process deployments.
that will be called on each run. Keep it idempotent: compute a
and check whether it already exists in your store before doing any work.
(SQLAlchemy or Redis) so the schedule survives process restarts. Without a persistent job store, a restarted process starts fresh and may fire missed runs immediately.
with a cron or interval trigger. Set
to control how long after the scheduled time the job is still allowed to run if the scheduler was down.
to emit metrics, send alerts, or push failed jobs to a dead-letter table.
in a background thread (non-blocking) or as a standalone process, and keep the main process alive.
In unit tests, do not wait for real time to pass. Use
to advance the next run immediately, or mock the clock.

import hashlib
import anthropic
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from apscheduler.schedulers.blocking import BlockingScheduler
from apscheduler.jobstores.sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemyJobStore
from apscheduler.events import EVENT_JOB_ERROR, EVENT_JOB_EXECUTED

client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env

# --- Idempotency helper ---
completed_runs: set[str] = set()  # replace with a DB table in production

def run_id(job_name: str, scheduled_at: datetime) -> str:
    key = f"{job_name}:{scheduled_at.isoformat()}"
    return hashlib.sha256(key.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

# --- Agent function ---
def daily_summary_agent():
    """Summarise yesterday's support tickets."""
    scheduled_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc).replace(
        hour=0, minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0
    )
    rid = run_id("daily-summary", scheduled_at)

    if rid in completed_runs:
        print(f"[{rid}] already completed, skipping")
        return

    # Fetch data (replace with real DB query)
    tickets = ["Customer can't log in", "Payment failing for EU users"]
    ticket_text = "\n".join(f"- {t}" for t in tickets)

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Summarise these support tickets from {scheduled_at.date()} "
                    f"into 3 bullet points:\n\n{ticket_text}"
                ),
            }
        ],
    )
    summary = response.content[0].text

    # Persist result (replace with real DB write)
    print(f"[{rid}] Summary:\n{summary}")
    completed_runs.add(rid)  # mark run as done

# --- Error listener ---
def on_job_event(event):
    if event.exception:
        print(f"Job {event.job_id} failed: {event.exception}")
        # emit to PagerDuty / Slack / dead-letter table here
    else:
        print(f"Job {event.job_id} executed successfully")

# --- Scheduler setup ---
jobstores = {
    "default": SQLAlchemyJobStore(url="sqlite:///jobs.db")  # persists across restarts
}

scheduler = BlockingScheduler(jobstores=jobstores)
scheduler.add_listener(on_job_event, EVENT_JOB_ERROR | EVENT_JOB_EXECUTED)

scheduler.add_job(
    daily_summary_agent,
    trigger="cron",
    hour=9,
    minute=0,
    id="daily-summary",
    replace_existing=True,
    misfire_grace_time=3600,  # run up to 1 hour late if scheduler was down
    max_instances=1,          # prevent overlapping runs
)

print("Scheduler started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.")
scheduler.start()

# tasks.py  (run worker: celery -A tasks worker --beat -l info)

import anthropic
from celery import Celery
from celery.schedules import crontab
from kombu.utils.uuid import uuid

app = Celery(
    "agent_jobs",
    broker="redis://localhost:6379/0",
    backend="redis://localhost:6379/1",
)

app.conf.beat_schedule = {
    "weekly-digest": {
        "task": "tasks.weekly_digest_agent",
        "schedule": crontab(hour=8, minute=0, day_of_week="monday"),
        "options": {"queue": "agent-scheduled"},
    },
    "hourly-ingest": {
        "task": "tasks.ingest_new_documents",
        "schedule": crontab(minute=0),  # every hour
        "options": {"queue": "agent-scheduled"},
    },
}
app.conf.timezone = "UTC"


@app.task(
    bind=True,
    autoretry_for=(Exception,),
    retry_backoff=True,       # exponential back-off between retries
    retry_kwargs={"max_retries": 3},
    acks_late=True,           # only ack after completion, not on pick-up
    task_id=uuid(),
)
def weekly_digest_agent(self):
    """Generate and email the weekly product digest."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Check idempotency (use task_id as the dedup key)
    task_id = self.request.id
    # (in production: check a DB table for this task_id)

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": "Write a 5-bullet weekly digest of our product updates.",
            }
        ],
    )
    digest = response.content[0].text

    # Send email / write to DB
    print(f"[{task_id}] Digest ready ({len(digest)} chars)")
    return {"task_id": task_id, "chars": len(digest)}


@app.task(
    autoretry_for=(Exception,),
    retry_backoff=True,
    retry_kwargs={"max_retries": 5},
)
def ingest_new_documents():
    """Pull new documents from source API and upsert into vector store."""
    # Fetch incremental batch since last run
    # Embed with your embedding model
    # Upsert into Pinecone / Weaviate / pgvector
    print("Ingested 0 new documents (stub)")

A SaaS company wants to identify customers at risk of churning each night. At 02:00 UTC every day, an agent pulls the previous day's usage events, calls Claude to classify each customer as low / medium / high churn risk, and writes a
record to a Postgres table. The CRM team's dashboard reads that table each morning.

import json
import hashlib
import anthropic
from datetime import date, datetime, timezone
from apscheduler.schedulers.blocking import BlockingScheduler
from apscheduler.jobstores.sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemyJobStore

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def get_usage_events(target_date: date) -> list[dict]:
    """Stub — replace with real DB query."""
    return [
        {"customer_id": "cust_001", "logins": 0, "api_calls": 2, "plan": "pro"},
        {"customer_id": "cust_002", "logins": 15, "api_calls": 340, "plan": "enterprise"},
        {"customer_id": "cust_003", "logins": 1, "api_calls": 0, "plan": "starter"},
    ]


def upsert_churn_risk(records: list[dict], run_date: date) -> None:
    """Stub — replace with real DB upsert."""
    for r in records:
        print(f"[{run_date}] {r['customer_id']}: {r['risk']} (reason: {r['reason']})")


def nightly_churn_classifier():
    today = datetime.now(timezone.utc).date()
    run_id = hashlib.sha256(f"churn-classifier:{today}".encode()).hexdigest()[:12]

    # Idempotency: skip if this date's run already completed
    # (in production, check a runs table by run_id)

    events = get_usage_events(today)
    if not events:
        print(f"[{run_id}] No events for {today}, skipping")
        return

    rows_json = json.dumps(events, indent=2)
    prompt = (
        f"You are a churn-risk classifier. Given the following customer usage events "
        f"for {today}, classify each customer as low, medium, or high churn risk. "
        f"Return a JSON array with objects: {{\"customer_id\", \"risk\", \"reason\"}}. "
        f"No prose, only valid JSON.\n\nEvents:\n{rows_json}"
    )

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    raw = response.content[0].text.strip()

    try:
        classified = json.loads(raw)
    except json.JSONDecodeError as exc:
        raise RuntimeError(f"LLM returned non-JSON: {raw[:200]}") from exc

    upsert_churn_risk(classified, today)
    print(f"[{run_id}] Classified {len(classified)} customers for {today}")


scheduler = BlockingScheduler(
    jobstores={"default": SQLAlchemyJobStore(url="sqlite:///churn.db")}
)
scheduler.add_job(
    nightly_churn_classifier,
    trigger="cron",
    hour=2,
    minute=0,
    id="churn-classifier",
    replace_existing=True,
    misfire_grace_time=7200,  # tolerate up to 2 h of downtime
    max_instances=1,
)
scheduler.start()
If a job crashes after the LLM call but before persisting results, a retry will call the LLM again — doubling cost and potentially emailing duplicate reports. Always write a
to a durable store before doing expensive work, and skip on re-entry.
prevents overlap, but only within a single process. Celery Beat distributes runs across workers — use a distributed lock (Redis
extension) to prevent concurrent runs across instances.
on error hides failures from your monitoring stack. Let exceptions propagate so the scheduler records them; attach a listener to forward errors to your alerting system.

is not set (defaults to 1 second in APScheduler), any run that starts more than one second late is silently discarded. Set it to a duration appropriate for your deployment (e.g., 1–2 hours for nightly jobs).

A single-process APScheduler is not suitable for high-availability deployments. It has no replication, no leader election, and no horizontal scaling. Use Celery Beat (single Beat process + many workers) or a managed scheduler (AWS EventBridge, GCP Cloud Scheduler, Temporal) for production.
Hardcoded API keys in job code:

Scheduled jobs often run in automated contexts where developers forget to inject secrets properly. Always read credentials from environment variables or a secrets manager — never embed them in source code.
Design every job to be idempotent
from the start. Compute a stable
before any side-effecting work, check it, and use upsert semantics in your database rather than bare inserts.
on retries. A flat retry interval causes thundering herds when many jobs fail simultaneously (e.g., during an LLM API outage). Celery's
timestamp to your job-state table every 30–60 seconds. A separate watchdog process or health-check endpoint can detect stalled jobs and alert before they exhaust their retry budget.
as Prometheus counters. Set an alert if a job has not succeeded within 1.5× its expected interval.
After max retries, move failed jobs to a separate table or queue rather than discarding them. This enables manual replay, forensic debugging, and SLA breach reporting.
Separate schedule definition from business logic:

Keep job functions pure (no scheduler imports). The scheduler wraps them. This makes jobs testable without starting the scheduler, and lets you migrate schedulers (APScheduler → Temporal) without rewriting business logic.
Freeze the clock in unit tests using
. Never sleep for real seconds in test suites.
A single APScheduler process handles tens of jobs comfortably. When you need higher throughput or fault tolerance, scale along two axes:

With Celery Beat, a single Beat process handles scheduling (it is intentionally not replicated) while you run many worker processes. Each worker competes for tasks on the same queue. Increasing worker count directly increases how many jobs can run in parallel.
with Celery's asyncio or gevent pool to run multiple jobs per worker process simultaneously. A single CPU core can saturate several in-flight LLM requests.

For jobs that classify or process many records, send multiple items in one prompt rather than one LLM call per record. This reduces API round-trips and often improves quality through cross-item context. Check your provider's token-per-minute limits and batch size accordingly.
When running multiple Beat-equivalent processes (for fault tolerance), use Redis-based leader election (e.g., Redlock) so only one process fires each job at a time.

Schedule heavy jobs at off-peak hours to stay within LLM API rate limits. Stagger cron times across jobs — if all five daily reports fire at 09:00:00 exactly, they will compete for the same tokens-per-minute budget. Offset them by 5–10 minutes each.
Workers: yes. Beat: single process (no HA out of the box)
Nightly reports, hourly ingestion, periodic sweeps
HTTP request from user or service
Interactive chat, query answering, user-facing features
Long document generation with live progress

or database credentials in job code. Inject them at runtime via environment variables, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, or Vault. Rotate secrets without redeploying by pulling from the secrets store on each job invocation.
The service account or IAM role running scheduled jobs should have only the permissions needed for that job. A nightly report agent should not have write access to production infrastructure.
Write a record per job run —
— to a durable table. This is essential for debugging missed runs, proving SLA adherence, and forensic investigation of incorrect outputs.
in your worker process. Allow in-progress jobs to complete before exiting (or checkpoint and retry). Abrupt kills leave jobs in an ambiguous state that your idempotency check must handle on restart.

Parse and validate LLM output before persisting it. If the model returns malformed JSON or an unexpected schema, raise an exception and let the retry mechanism handle it rather than writing garbage to your database.
Set an explicit wall-clock timeout on every job, slightly shorter than the schedule interval. A stuck job that holds a distributed lock indefinitely will block all future runs. Celery's
Scheduled jobs make agents proactive: they run at a fixed time or interval without any user request, enabling nightly reports, hourly ingestion, and periodic monitoring.
Every scheduled agent job must be idempotent. Compute a stable
, write it to a durable store before side effects, and skip on re-entry.
or a Redis lock in Celery) to prevent concurrent runs when a job takes longer than its interval.

Instrument every job: track duration, success, and failure as time-series metrics; alert if a job has not succeeded within 1.5× its expected interval; route failed runs to a dead-letter queue for manual replay.

APScheduler suits single-process prototypes; Celery Beat scales to multi-worker deployments; Temporal Schedules add durable replay and fine-grained overlap control for high-reliability production systems.
Stagger cron times across jobs by 5–10 minutes each to avoid simultaneous bursts that exhaust LLM API rate limits at the same second.
Separate job business logic from scheduler infrastructure: keep job functions pure and testable without importing the scheduler. Freeze the clock in tests using
— never sleep for real seconds in test suites.
